document.title: Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejections of Imported Foods
document.label: CXG 25-1997 - Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejections of Imported Foods

sections.title: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: The following guidelines provide the basis for structured information exchange on rejections of imported food where the reason for the rejection is related to food safety and fair practices in food trade. These guidelines apply where food has been refused entry to a country due to a failure to comply with importing country requirements. Where a food safety emergency situation has been identified, the Guidelines for the Exchange of Information in Food Safety Emergency Situations (CAC/GL 19-1995) should be used. The use of these guidelines is intended to improve transparency where food is rejected and to build on: – the Principles for Food Import and Export Inspection and Certification (CAC/GL 20 -1995), in particular the transparency provisions contained in paragraph 15 of the Principles – the Guidelines for Food Import Control Systems (CAC/GL 47-2003), in particular the decisions and information exchange provisions contained in paragraphs 27 -29 and 34 of the guidelines respectively.

INTRODUCTION
number=1
level=1
The following guidelines provide the basis for structured information exchange on rejections of imported food where the reason for the rejection is related to food safety and fair practices in food trade. These guidelines apply where food has been refused entry to a country due to a failure to comply with importing country requirements. Where a food safety emergency situation has been identified, the Guidelines for the Exchange of Information in Food Safety Emergency Situations (CAC/GL 19-1995) should be used. The use of these guidelines is intended to improve transparency where food is rejected and to build on: – the Principles for Food Import and Export Inspection and Certification (CAC/GL 20 -1995), in particular the transparency provisions contained in paragraph 15 of the Principles – the Guidelines for Food Import Control Systems (CAC/GL 47-2003), in particular the decisions and information exchange provisions contained in paragraphs 27 -29 and 34 of the guidelines respectively.

Where a food safety emergency situation has been identified, the Guidelines for the Exchange of Information in Food Safety Emergency Situations (CAC/GL 19-1995) should be used. The use of these guidelines is intended to improve transparency where food is rejected and to build on: – the Principles for Food Import and Export Inspection and Certification (CAC/GL 20 -1995), in particular the transparency provisions contained in paragraph 15 of the Principles – the Guidelines for Food Import Control Systems (CAC/GL 47-2003), in particular the decisions and information exchange provisions contained in paragraphs 27 -29 and 34 of the guidelines respectively.

sections.title: GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
sections.text: Rejections may occur where the competent authority of the importing country has identified that the consignment does not comply with importing country requirements, which may include: – evidence the consignment presents a food safety risk – evidence that the consignment has been compromised during handling, storage or transport – evidence of misrepresentation or consumer fraud. When the competent authority rejects a consignment of food presented for importation due to non-compliance with importing country requirements, information should be exchanged to advise relevant parties of the rejection, to enable relevant parties to attain any necessary clarifications, and where appropriate implement corrective and preventative measures. Where appropriate, information should be provided to the competent authority of the exporting country (or embassy if the competent authority is not known) and the importer and/or exporter of the rejected consignment. Where appropriate, the competent authority of the exporting country should have reasonable access to the evidence found by the importing country, so as to be able to investigate the cause of the non-compliance and implement and manage any corrective actions as required. If requested, the competent authority in the exporting country should provide the competent authority in the importing country with information on the outcome of the necessary investigations and corrective actions taken. Based on the information provided, in accordance with the importing country’s legislation, the importer and/or exporter, in consultation with the competent authority of the importing and exporting countries as appropriate, may determine what action to take. Where there is evidence of repeated failures of a correctable nature that are not associated with food safety (e.g. labelling errors, mislaying of documents) or there have been systematic failures, the competent authority in the importing country may also make appropriate notification to the competent authority in the exporting country, either periodically or upon request. Bilateral discussions should take place as necessary between the competent authorities of the importing and exporting countries regarding information of rejected food. As far as possible, importing countries should minimise restrictions on the disclosure to exporting countries of information on rejected foods.

When the competent authority rejects a consignment of food presented for importation due to non-compliance with importing country requirements, information should be exchanged to advise relevant parties of the rejection, to enable relevant parties to attain any necessary clarifications, and where appropriate implement corrective and preventative measures. Where appropriate, information should be provided to the competent authority of the exporting country (or embassy if the competent authority is not known) and the importer and/or exporter of the rejected consignment. Where appropriate, the competent authority of the exporting country should have reasonable access to the evidence found by the importing country, so as to be able to investigate the cause of the non-compliance and implement and manage any corrective actions as required. If requested, the competent authority in the exporting country should provide the competent authority in the importing country with information on the outcome of the necessary investigations and corrective actions taken. source.document_title: Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejections of Imported Foods

reference.document: CXG 25-1997 - Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejections of Imported Foods
reference.document_title: Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejections of Imported Foods

sections.title: DETAILED INFORMATION
sections.text:
Information exchange should be: 
– transmitted electronically to all relevant parties wherever possible  
– transparent, structured and timely to ensure rapid resolution and so alternative actions may be taken wherever possible 
– made in the language of the importing country, English or a third language, as mutually agreed. 
The reason(s) why a consignment of food has been rejected should be clearly stated and reference should be made to the regulations or standards which have been contravened. A clear description of the criteria for rejection should be provided to ensure transparency. Details on the type of information to exchange are provided in Annex 1. 
Where a consignment is rejected on the basis of analysis performed in the importing country, the competent authority of the importing country should make available , upon request, details of the sampling and analytical methods employed, the results obtained and the details of the testing laboratory. 
Where the level of a contaminant has been found to be above the maximum permitted level, the contaminant should be specified, together with the level found and the maximum permitted level. In the case of biological contamination or contamination by biological toxins, where no maximum level has been fixed, the identity of the organism or toxin should be given as specifically as possible, and as appropriate, the level of contamination found.  
Contraventions of regulations on food additive or compositional standards should be specified.  
Some countries accept certain foods (e.g. fresh meat) only from specifically approved establishments in the exporting country. If such foods are refused entry because of evidence that they come from such an establishment is lacking or incomplete, this should be stated.

sections.title: Action taken
sections.text:
Information on action taken should be provided, such as: 
– Food destroyed  
– Food held pending reconditioning/rectification of deficiencies in documentation  
– Import granted for use other than human consumption  
– Re-export granted under certain conditions, e.g. to specified informed countries  
– Importer notified  
– Embassy/food control authorities of exporting country notified  
– Authorities in other likely destination countries notified  
– Other

sections.title: Reason(s) for rejection
sections.text:
– Incomplete or incorrect certification 
– Does not come from an approved country, region or establishment  
– Food adulteration 
– Other reasons

reference.document_title: Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejections of Imported Foods
reference.section_id: path_titles::reason(s) for rejection::lvl1

sentences.original: The following guidelines provide the basis for structured information exchange on rejections of imported food where the reason for the rejection is related to food safety and fair practices in food trade.

prefilter.reason: This sentence discusses the purpose of guidelines and references specific standards, typical for regulatory context.

classification.sentence: The use of these guidelines is intended to improve transparency where food is rejected and to build on: – the Principles for Food Import and Export Inspection and Certification (CAC/GL 20 -1995), in particular the transparency provisions contained in paragraph 15 of the Principles – the Guidelines for Food Import Control Systems (CAC/GL 47-2003), in particular the decisions and information exchange provisions contained in paragraphs 27 -29 and 34 of the guidelines respectively.
classification.function: Explain intended purpose and related documents
classification.pattern: Declarative sentence with infinitive purpose clauses and enumerated list

components.purpose: improve transparency and build on named guidelines

classification.justification: This descriptive sentence explains intention and references related documents without normative force or specific obligation/permission; no template matched.

sentences.original: These guidelines apply where food has been refused entry to a country due to a failure to comply with importing country requirements.

prefilter.reason: This sentence discusses the purpose of guidelines and references specific standards, typical for regulatory context.

sentences.original: Rejections may occur where the competent authority of the importing country has identified that the consignment does not comply with importing country requirements, which may include: – evidence the consignment presents a food safety risk – evidence that the consignment has been compromised during handling, storage or transport – evidence of misrepresentation or consumer fraud.

classification.sentence: As far as possible, importing countries should minimise restrictions on the disclosure to exporting countries of information on rejected foods.
classification.function: Mandate minimization of restrictions with qualification
classification.pattern: Adverbial phrase + subject + modal 'should' + verb phrase + object phrase

components.obligation: importing countries should minimise restrictions on disclosure

classification.justification: Qualified obligation to minimize restrictions; no close template match, so new pattern.

reference.document_title: Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejections of Imported Foods

sentences.original: When the competent authority rejects a consignment of food presented for importation due to non-compliance with importing country requirements, information should be exchanged to advise relevant parties of the rejection, to enable relevant parties to attain any necessary clarifications, and where appropriate implement corrective and preventative measures.

classification.sentence: As far as possible, importing countries should minimise restrictions on the disclosure to exporting countries of information on rejected foods.
classification.function: Mandate minimization of restrictions with qualification
classification.pattern: Adverbial phrase + subject + modal 'should' + verb phrase + object phrase

components.obligation: importing countries should minimise restrictions on disclosure

classification.justification: Qualified obligation to minimize restrictions; no close template match, so new pattern.

sentences.original: Where there is evidence of repeated failures of a correctable nature that are not associated with food safety (e.g. labelling errors, mislaying of documents) or there have been systematic failures, the competent authority in the importing country may also make appropriate notification to the competent authority in the exporting country, either periodically or upon request.

sentences.original:
Information exchange should be: 
– transmitted electronically to all relevant parties wherever possible  
– transparent, structured and timely to ensure rapid resolution and so alternative actions may be taken wherever possible 
– made in the language of the importing country, English or a third language, as mutually agreed. 
The reason(s) why a consignment of food has been rejected should be clearly stated and reference should be made to the regulations or standards which have been contravened.

classification.sentence: If such foods are refused entry because of evidence that they come from such an establishment is lacking or incomplete, this should be stated.
classification.function: Mandate stating refusal reason upon condition
classification.pattern: Conditional ('If' clause) + main clause with modal 'should' + passive verb

components.condition: foods refused entry due to lack or incomplete evidence from approved establishment

classification.justification: Conditional normative obligation triggered by evidence status; new pattern classified due to lack of template match.

classification.sentence: Information on the following should be provided:

sentences.original: The following information should be provided by countries in relation to rejections of imported food, as available and appropriate to the circumstances.

classification.sentence: The following information should be provided by countries in relation to rejections of imported food, as available and appropriate to the circumstances.
classification.function: Instruction/requirement to provide specified information with conditional qualification
classification.pattern: Declarative with modal 'should' and qualifying phrases indicating conditional provision

components.context: in relation to rejections of imported food
components.qualifications: as available and appropriate to the circumstances

classification.justification: Obligation modality with qualifications limiting scope; instructs provision of information; no match in known templates catalog.

sentences.original:
Information on action taken should be provided, such as: 
– Food destroyed  
– Food held pending reconditioning/rectification of deficiencies in documentation  
– Import granted for use other than human consumption  
– Re-export granted under certain conditions, e.g. to specified informed countries  
– Importer notified  
–

prefilter.reason: Mentions notification in destination countries, relevant to regulatory process.

classification.sentence:
– Authorities in other likely destination countries notified  
– Other
classification.function: Continuation of instructions listing notified authorities or alternatives
classification.pattern: List items indicating actions or options in parallel

list: Authorities in other likely destination countries notified

classification.justification: No modal verb but understood as continuation of obligation to notify; list structure; no exact match in templates.

pages.title: GUIDELINES FOR THE EXCHANGE OF INFORMATION BETWEEN COUNTRIES ON REJECTIONS OF IMPORTED FOOD
pages.summary: Página contém o título principal do documento e o código no cabeçalho. Não há seções, subtítulos ou texto interno identificáveis nesta página.
Página com duas seções principais numeradas 1. INTRODUCTION e 2. GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS, com seus textos completos, sem indicações de continuidade entre páginas.
A página contém uma seção principal numerada 3. DETAILED INFORMATION com seu texto completo na página. Não há indicações claras de continuidade para outras páginas.

pages.title: CAC/GL 25-1997
pages.summary: Esta página contém o ANEXO I do documento com título principal CAC/GL 25-1997. O anexo apresenta uma seção principal sem numeração com vários subtítulos também sem numeração explícita, todos em nível hierárquico 1 devido à formatação de títulos em caixa alta e linhas curtas. O texto associado a cada seção foi segmentado conforme o conteúdo apresentado, preservando a ordem e a semântica. A seção 'Reason(s) for rejection' indica continuidade para a página seguinte, mantendo a coerência do conteúdo para RAG.

pages.title: CAC/GL 25-1997
pages.summary: Página contém duas seções principais sem numeração explicita: 'Reason(s) for rejection' e 'Action taken', ambas em nível 1. A seção 'Reason(s) for rejection' continua da página anterior e continua para a próxima, marcada para continuidade, enquanto 'Action taken' é nova nesta página e completa. Texto foi segmentado conforme os subtítulos, preservando ordem e clareza para RAG.
